slides.title: Write Once, Use Anywhere
slides.body: A **function** is a named set of instructions you define once and run whenever you need. It's how you teach Python a brand-new trick — no copy-pasting required.
slides.tip: This idea has a name: DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself.

slides.title: Anatomy of `def`
slides.body: Use `def`, a name, parentheses, and a colon — then **indent** the body. Python owns everything indented underneath.
slides.tip: Don't forget the colon `:` — it's the most common beginner slip.

slides.title: Pass Data In with Parameters
slides.body: Add **parameters** inside the parentheses so your function can work with different inputs each time it's called.
slides.code:
def greet(name):
    print("Hello,", name)

greet("Alice")
greet("Bob")
slides.tip: Parameter = placeholder in the definition. Argument = real value you pass in.

slides.title: Document It with a Docstring
slides.body: Drop a **triple-quoted string** as the very first line of your function to explain what it does — future-you will be grateful.
slides.tip: Even one short sentence is enough. Write it in plain English.

slides.title: You've Got Functions!
slides.body: You can now define functions with `def`, accept inputs via parameters, and document them with docstrings. Every Python program you'll ever write uses these building blocks.
slides.tip: Next up: making functions hand data back using `return`.
